items.title: Complete
items.contentHtml:
Biological cycle of E. histolytica

Biological cycle

  • Tetranucleated cyst (infective form) fecal-oral route.
  • In the small intestine it excysts → metacystic trophozoite → colonizes colon.
  • Trophozoite multiplies by fission binary in colonic lumen.
  • Formation of uninucleated cyst (pre-cyst) and then mature tetranucleated cyst eliminated in feces.
  • Direct cycle; does not need an intermediate host.


Mechanism of tissue invasion

Pathogenic invader

  • Adhesion lectin (Gal/GalNAc) mediates adhesion to colonic mucin.
  • Amoebapore forms pores in the membrane of epithelial cells and phagocytes.
  • Cysteine proteinases degrade extracellular matrix and evade immune response.
  • Trophozoite induces apoptosis of enterocytes and neutrophils.
  • Invasion of mesenteric capillaries → portal dissemination → liver.


differential diagnosis E. histolytica vs E. disparate

Key differentiation

  • E. histolyticapathogenic (invasive); E. dispar commensal (non-invasive).
  • Identical morphology in microscopy: they cannot be differentiated.
  • Detection of antigen in feces (ELISA) specific for E. histolytica.
  • PCR in feces is the gold standard for differentiation.
  • Trophozoites with phagocytosed erythrocytes only in E. histolytica (pathognomonic) E. dispar).
  • It is observed in acute amoebic dysentery.
  • Sample should be examined within 30 minutes (labile trophozoite).
  • Low sensitivity (≈30-50%); requires multiple serial samples.


Ameboma

Ameboma

  • Inflammatory granulomatous mass in colon (cecum or sigmoid).
  • Clinic: abdominal pain, palpable mass, constipation (not dysentery).
  • differential diagnosis with colon carcinoma (colonoscopy + biopsy).
  • Image: "baton handle" stenosis in barium enema.
  • Treatment: tissue amoebicide (metronidazole) → resolution in 2-4 weeks.


Amebic liver abscess vs. pyogenic

Differential diagnosis

  • Amebic abscess: fever, HD pain, chocolate pus (“anchovies in sauce”), sterile culture, positive serology.
  • Pyogenic abscess: high fever, jaundice, purulent pus, positive culture (E. coli, anaerobes).
  • Image: single hypoechoic lesion, right lobe in amoebic; multiple or septated in pyogenic.
  • Treatment: amoebic → metronidazole ± drainage if >10 cm or risk of rupture.


Metronidazole: treatment of choice

Metronidazole

  • tissue amoebicide (active against trophozoites in tissue) diloxanide).
  • Adverse effects: metallic taste, nausea, neuropathy (avoid alcohol).


Complications: perforation and peritonitis

Amebic perforation

  • Colonic perforation in patients with severe dysentery or immunocompromised.
  • Clinic: sudden abdominal pain, peritoneal signs, pneumoperitoneum (plain radiography).
  • High mortality (>50%) if not operated.
  • Treatment: emergency laparotomy + resection + drainage + intravenous metronidazole.
  • Differential diagnosis: inflammatory bowel disease (avoid steroids).


Prevention with sanitary measures

Prevention

  • Washing hands after defecating and before eating.
  • Consume drinking water (boiled or treated).
  • Wash and peel raw fruits/vegetables.
  • Avoid ice in endemic areas.
  • No vaccine available; basic sanitation is the most effective measure.


Asymptomatic carrier and paromomycin

Management of the carrier

  • Asymptomatic carrier: eliminates cysts of E. histolytica (not E. dispar).
  • Treatment: paromomycin 25-35 mg/kg/d PO in 3 doses x 7 days.
  • Alternative: diloxanide furoate 500 mg every 8h x 10 days.
  • Do not use metronidazole in asymptomatic patients (it does not eradicate cysts).
  • Confirm eradication with 3 negative stool cultures post-treatment.

items.title: Life cycle of E. histolytica
items.contentHtml:
Biological cycle

  • Tetranucleated cyst (infective form) fecal-oral route.
  • In the small intestine excysts → metacystic trophozoite → colonizes colon.
  • Trophozoite multiplies by binary fission in colonic lumen.
  • Formation of uninucleated cyst (pre-cyst) and then mature tetranucleated cyst eliminated in feces.
  • Direct cycle; It does not need an intermediary host.

items.title: Mechanism of tissue invasion
items.contentHtml:
Pathogenic invader

  • Adhesion lectin (Gal/GalNAc) mediates adhesion to colonic mucin.
  • Amebapore forms pores in the membrane of epithelial cells and phagocytes.
  • Cysteine proteinases degrade extracellular matrix and evade immune response.
  • Trophozoite induces apoptosis of enterocytes and neutrophils.
  • Invasion of mesenteric capillaries → portal spread → liver.

items.title: Differential diagnosis: E. histolytica vs E. dispar
items.contentHtml:
Key differentiation

  • E. histolyticapathogenic (invasive); E. dispar commensal (non-invasive).
  • Identical morphology in microscopy: they cannot be differentiated.
  • Detection of antigen in feces (ELISA) specific for E. histolytica.
  • PCR in feces is the gold standard for differentiation.
  • Trophozoites with phagocytosed erythrocytes only in E. histolytica (pathognomonic).

items.title: Hematophagous trophozoites
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic finding

  • [Key finding]: Trophozoite with phagocytosed erythrocytes in fresh or fresh feces.
  • Indicates invasive infection by E. histolytica (never in E. dispar).
  • It is observed in acute amoebic dysentery.
  • Sample should be examined within 30 minutes (labile trophozoite).
  • Low sensitivity (≈30-50%); requires multiple serial samples.

items.title: Ameboma
items.contentHtml:
Ameboma

  • Inflammatory granulomatous mass in the colon (cecum or sigmoid).
  • Clinic: abdominal pain, palpable mass, constipation (not dysentery).
  • differential diagnosis with colon carcinoma (colonoscopy + biopsy).
  • Image: stenosis in “baton handle” in barium enema.
  • Treatment: tissue amoebicide (metronidazole) → resolution in 2-4 weeks.

items.title: Amoebic vs pyogenic liver abscess
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis

  • Amebic abscess: fever, HD pain, chocolate pus (“anchovies in sauce”), sterile culture, positive serology.
  • Pyogenic abscess: high fever, jaundice, purulent pus, positive culture (E. coli, anaerobes).
  • Image: single hypoechoic lesion, right lobe in amoebic; multiple or septated in pyogenic.
  • Treatment: amoebic → metronidazole ± drainage if >10 cm or risk of rupture.

items.title: Metronidazole: treatment of choice
items.contentHtml:
Metronidazole

  • Tissue amebicide (active against trophozoites in tissue).
  • Dose: 750 mg PO every 8h x 10 days (adult) or 35-50 mg/kg/d in children.
  • Eliminates invasive forms, but does not eradicate cysts. luminal.
  • Requires posterior luminal amoebicide (paromomycin or diloxanide).
  • Adverse effects: metallic taste, nausea, neuropathy (avoid alcohol).

items.title: Complications: perforation and peritonitis
items.contentHtml:
Amebic perforation

  • Colonic perforation in patients with severe dysentery or immunocompromised.
  • Clinic: sudden abdominal pain, peritoneal signs, pneumoperitoneum (plain radiography).
  • High mortality (>50%) if not opera.
  • Treatment: emergency laparotomy + resection + drainage + intravenous metronidazole.
  • Differential diagnosis: inflammatory bowel disease (avoid steroids).

items.title: Prevention with sanitary measures
items.contentHtml:
Prevention

  • Washing hands after defecating and before eating.
  • Consume drinking water (boiled or treated).
  • Wash and peel raw fruits/vegetables.
  • Avoid ice in endemic areas.
  • There is no vaccine available; basic sanitation is the most effective measure.
